cards.back: The study of motion without regard to its causes.
The study of forces and their effect on motion.
The location of an object relative to a coordinate system.
Change in position: $\Delta x = x_f - x_0$.
Total distance divided by total time: $v_{avg} = \frac{d}{t}$.
Displacement divided by time: $\bar{v} = \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$.
Velocity at a specific instant: $v = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}$.

cards.front: What does the **slope** of a position-time graph give?
What does the **slope** of a velocity-time graph give?
What does the **area under** a velocity-time graph give?

cards.back: Rate of change of velocity: $a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$.
When velocity and acceleration have the same sign.
When velocity and acceleration have opposite signs.
Acceleration that causes a decrease in speed.

cards.front: What condition is required to use the kinematic equations?

cards.back: Motion under the influence of gravity only.

cards.front: What is the **acceleration due to gravity** ($g$)?
What is the acceleration of an object at the **top of its path**?

cards.back: Velocity at the end of the time interval.

cards.front: What does a **horizontal line** on a velocity-time graph mean?
What does a **horizontal line** on a position-time graph mean?
What does a **straight diagonal line** on a position-time graph mean?
What does a **parabolic curve** on a position-time graph mean?
What is the relationship between **instantaneous speed and instantaneous velocity**?
cards.back: Instantaneous speed is the magnitude of instantaneous velocity.

cards.front: Can an object have **zero velocity but non-zero acceleration**?
cards.back: Yes (e.g., at the top of free fall).

cards.front: Can an object have **constant velocity but changing speed**?
cards.back: No — constant velocity means constant speed and direction.

cards.front: What is the **area under** an acceleration-time graph?
What happens to velocity when acceleration is **constant and positive**?
What is the **SI unit for acceleration**?
What does the **slope of an acceleration-time graph** represent?
